hi.heading: उदाहरण
hi.content: Dekho — tumhare papa ya mummy jab dukaan pe samaan khareedne jaate hain na — tab dukaan mein paison ka hisaab kaise rakha jaata hai, socho zara? Agar dukaan ko ₹500 ka fayda hua, toh wo +500 hai. Aur agar kisi din dukaan ko ₹200 ka nuksaan ho gaya, toh wo -200 hai. Ye +500 aur -200 hi toh Integers hain!

hi.heading: गलतफहमी
hi.wrong: Ek bahut common galti jo tum karti ho, wo ye hai ki kai baar tum sochti ho ki -10, -5 se bada hai, kyunki 10 toh 5 se bada hota hai na?
hi.correct: Lekin asal mein aisa nahi hota. Integers ko number line par dekho. Jo number number line par right side mein hota hai, wo bada hota hai. Aur jo left side mein hota hai, wo chhota hota hai. Toh -5, -10 se right mein hai, isliye -5 bada hai!

hi.heading: गतिविधि
hi.content: Ek kaam karo — ghar mein kuch laal bindi (bindi ke packet se) aur kuch neele bindi le lo. Har laal bindi ko +1 maano aur har neeli bindi ko -1. Jab bhi ek laal aur ek neeli bindi ek saath ho, toh socho ki wo ek doosre ko cancel kar dete hain (zero ban jaate hain). Ab '5 + (-3)' solve karne ke liye 5 laal bindi aur 3 neeli bindi lo — dekho kya bachta hai!

en.heading: Example
en.content: Hey didi/bhaiya, have you ever thought about how temperatures are talked about in places like Leh or Shimla during winter? When it's super cold, they say 'minus 5 degrees Celsius' or '-5°C'. 'Above freezing point' is a positive temperature, like +2°C. But 'below freezing point' is negative, like -5°C. These positive and negative numbers are exactly what we call Integers!

en.heading: Misconception
en.wrong: A common mistake I often see is when students think that -10 is greater than -5, just because 10 is greater than 5.
en.correct: But actually, that's not true! Think about the number line. Numbers to the right are always greater, and numbers to the left are smaller. So, -5 is to the right of -10 on the number line, which means -5 is actually greater than -10.

en.heading: Activity
en.content: Let's try a fun little experiment at home! Collect some small red buttons and some small blue buttons. Let each red button represent +1 and each blue button represent -1. Now, whenever you have one red and one blue button together, imagine they cancel each other out (they become zero!). To solve '5 + (-3)', take 5 red buttons and 3 blue buttons — see what's left!

hi.heading: उदाहरण
hi.content: Dekho — tumhari Mummy jab halwa banati hain, toh kabhi-kabhi woh kehti hain, 'Aaj main sirf aadha (1/2) cup sooji use karungi.' Ab agar recipe mein 1 cup sooji ka ek chauthaai (1/4) ghee daalne ko kaha hai, toh socho zara — jab Mummy sirf aadha cup sooji use karengi, toh ghee bhi 'aadha cup sooji ke 1/4' ke hisaab se daalengi na? Toh us hisaab se, Mummy kitna ghee daalengi?

hi.heading: गलतफहमी
hi.wrong: Kai baar tum sochte ho ki 0.50 aur 0.9 mein se 0.50 bada hai, kyunki 'pachaas' 'nau' se bada lagta hai na?
hi.correct: Lekin beta, decimal numbers ko compare karte waqt humein unki place value dekhni hoti hai. 0.9 ko hum 0.90 bhi likh sakte hain, toh ab batao — 0.50 aur 0.90 mein se kaun bada hai? Har digit ki position dekho, units place, tenths place, hundredths place.

hi.heading: गतिविधि
hi.content: Ek kaam karo — ek square ya rectangle paper lo. Usko beech se fold karke aadhe-aadhe hisson mein baant do, phir dobara fold karke aur chhote hisse banao. Dekho, har fold ke saath kitne parts ban rahe hain aur har part poore paper ka kitna fraction hai?

en.heading: Example
en.content: Hey, remember when your mom makes those delicious laddoos during festivals? Sometimes, she has a big batch of mixture, say 1 kg. And she decides to use only half (1/2) of that mixture for one type of laddoo, and then she tells you, 'Beta, take one-third (1/3) of *that half* for yourself!' — So, you're not taking 1/3 of the whole 1 kg, but 1/3 of the 1/2 kg she set aside, right? How much of the total mixture did you actually get then?

en.heading: Misconception
en.wrong: A lot of times, students think that 0.19 is a bigger number than 0.2, because in whole numbers, nineteen is definitely bigger than two, isn't it?
en.correct: But darling, when we compare decimals, we need to look at the place value of each digit. Think of 0.2 as 0.20. Now, which is bigger when you compare 0.19 and 0.20? Always compare digits from left to right, starting with the tenths place.

en.heading: Activity
en.content: Here's something fun you can do — take any piece of paper, maybe a rectangular one. First, fold it exactly in half. Then fold it in half again, and maybe one more time. When you unfold it, how many equal parts did you make? Can you figure out what fraction each small part is of the whole paper?
